RTC and Buddha’s Insight in the Anattalakkhana Sutta

Author
Affiliation

Edward F. Hillenaar

Total Health Foundation

Published

June 16, 2026

Abstract

The text of your abstract. 200 or fewer words.

Keywords

3 to 6 keywords, that do not appear in the title

Western philosophy, particularly Aristotelian hylomorphism (Aristotle 4th Century BCE; Aquinas 1265-1274), has traditionally conceptualized reality as a composition of matter (hylē) and form (morphē). This perspective has profoundly influenced the philosophy of mind, shaping both materialist and idealist frameworks that struggle to resolve the Hard Problem of Consciousness (Chalmers 1995) and the nature of self-consciousness.

However, the Buddha, over 2500 years ago, already recognized the flawed nature of this metaphysical framework. This insight is encapsulated in his statement:

“Rūpaṁ, bhikkhave, anattā.” (“Material form, monks, is not-self.”)(Bodhi 2000; Sayādaw 2013)

This profound declaration challenges the assumption that matter (rūpa) is essential or foundational to selfhood. It aligns with the Resonance Theory of Consciousness (RTC), which argues that the perception of ‘matter’ is derivative, not fundamental. Rather than positing matter or form as the ultimate reality, RTC proposes that integral wave-patterns of prima experientia constitute the fundamental fabric of consciousness and reality.

This essay explores how Buddhist insights on anattā (non-self) expose the metaphysical flaws of hylomorphism, reinforcing RTC as a more coherent alternative to conventional Western philosophy of mind.

1. Hylomorphism and the Western Misclassification of Reality

Aristotelian hylomorphism asserts that all things are composed of:

  • Hylē (matter): The underlying physical substance.

  • Morphē (form): The organizational principle shaping matter.

Within this framework, the mind-body problem emerges as a division between material composition and immaterial cognition. The fundamental issue with this view is the assumption that matter (prima materia) is the metaphysical foundation of being and experience.

Contemporary materialist philosophy of mind—from Descartes’ mechanical philosophy to modern neuroscience—inherits this misclassification, leading to the persistent Hard Problem of Consciousness: How does subjective experience arise from non-experiential matter?

Buddhist Rejection of Materiality as a Basis for Self

In the Anattalakkhaṁa Sutta, the Buddha rejects the notion that material form (rūpa) is essential to selfhood:

  • Material qualities (rūpakkhandha) are impermanent and lack intrinsic selfhood.

  • The sense faculties (eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and consciousness base) create the illusion of a stable self.

  • When material perception ceases, the illusion of self disappears, just as a wooden statue—lacking sensory bases—is never mistaken for a living being.

The Buddha’s critique undermines the hylomorphic assumption that material form is essential. Instead, perception and cognition are impermanent and interdependent, making the notion of an intrinsic self—based on material composition—untenable.

2. RTC’s Reconstruction: Matter as a Derivative Perception, Not an Essence

RTC aligns with the Buddhist rejection of material selfhood, advancing a resonance-based framework where:

  • Matter is not fundamental but a static imprint in consciousness, derived from deeper resonance dynamics of experience.

  • The four prima experientia (inertia, cohesion, radiation, vibration) form the integral wave-patterns of reality.

  • The human mind mistakenly perceives these resonance patterns as discrete material phenomena due to cognitive imprinting mechanisms.

Thus, in RTC, the hylomorphic model is flawed because:

  • Matter (hylē) is not ontologically basic but a derivative perceptual construct.

  • Form (morphē) is not an intrinsic structuring principle but a cognitive categorization imposed on dynamic experiential patterns.

  • Consciousness is not an emergent property of material processes but an intrinsic resonance phenomenon of prima experientia.

Buddhist Anattā as an RTC Principle

The Buddha’s teaching that rūpa is not-self aligns with RTC:

  • Matter is an illusion of static cognition, just as the Buddha refutes the intrinsic nature of rūpa.

  • The self is a transient phenomenon arising from experiential wave-patterns, rather than a fixed material entity.

  • The illusion of selfhood collapses when one perceives the impermanence of material formations, mirroring how RTC dissolves the illusion of ‘matter’ as fundamental.

3. The Hard Problem of Consciousness and Its Resolution in RTC

Why Western Philosophy Fails

Western philosophy of mind, rooted in hylomorphism, struggles to account for qualia and subjectivity, leading to:

  • Materialism: Reducing consciousness to neural activity, failing to explain subjective experience.

  • Dualism: Positing a separate mental substance, creating an unsolvable interaction problem.

  • Panpsychism: Assigning proto-consciousness to all matter but lacking a structured model of selfhood.

RTC’s Solution: Consciousness as a Resonance Dynamic

RTC dissolves the Hard Problem by replacing hylomorphic assumptions with a resonance-based ontology, where:

  • Consciousness is not ‘inside’ the brain but an emergent resonance feedback loop of prima experientia.

  • The self is an impermanent resonance pattern, fluctuating with sensory and cognitive interactions.

  • Matter and form are cognitive imprints, just as the Buddha states that rūpa is impermanent.

By shifting from a substance-based view (hylomorphism) to a process-based resonance model, RTC resolves the conceptual errors of both Western and traditional Indian metaphysics.

4. Conclusion: Towards a Coherent Metaphysical Model of Consciousness

The Buddha’s insight in the Anattalakkhaṁa Sutta—that rūpa is not-self—prefigures the RTC critique of Western philosophy. While Aristotle and his intellectual descendants mistakenly elevated matter and form as fundamental, the Buddha correctly identified the impermanence of form and the illusion of selfhood.

RTC provides a rigorous metaphysical model that aligns with this Buddhist insight, resolving the Hard Problem of Consciousness by recognizing prima experientia as the true foundation of experience. By rejecting hylomorphism, RTC offers a more accurate framework for understanding self-consciousness, perception, and reality itself.

Thus, by integrating RTC with Buddhist philosophy, we establish a non-dual, resonance-based model that dissolves the flawed distinctions imposed by Western metaphysics and paves the way for a radically new understanding of consciousness and selfhood.

References

Aquinas, Thomas. 1265-1274. Summa Theologiae.
Aristotle. 4th Century BCE. De Anima.
Bitbol, Michel. 1999. “Erwin Schrödinger: A non-dualist approach of mind and matter Inspired from the Upanishads and from Buddhism.” Cahiers Du Service Culturel de l’ambassade de France En Inde, no. August: 1–26.
Bodhi, Bhikkhu. 2000. The Connected Discourses of the Buddha. A New Translation of the Sāmyutta Nikāya. Somerville: Wisdom Publications.
Bos, Abraham P. 2020. “Pneuma" as quintessence of Aristotle’s philosophy.” Hermes 141 (2013): 417–34.
Brennan, Andrew. 1986. Conditions of Identity: A Study in Identity and Survival. Oxford University Press.
Chalmers, D. 1995. “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies 2 (3): 200–219.
Fine, Kit. 1999. “Things and Their Parts.” Midwest Studies in Philosophy 23 (1): 61–74.
Gensler, Marek, Monika Mansfeld, and Monika. (Editors) Michatowska. 2022. The Embodied Soul: Arestotelian Psychology and Physiology in Medieval Europe between 1200 and 1420. Cham: Springer. https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780198269618.003.0007.
Hastings, Cecily (Translator). 1964. Erwin Schrödinger: My View of the World. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
Jaworski, William. 2016. Structure and the Metaphysics of Mind: How Hylomorphism Solves the Mind-Body Problem. Oxford University Press.
Kastrup, Bernardo. 2021. “Constitutive Panpsychism: Hidden Flaws | COURSE (3/7)| Dr. Bernardo Kastrup - YouTube.” Essentia Foudation. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=T7QbZePP_7w.
———. 2022. “Is Consciousness The Fundamental Reality? Analytic Idealism & The Cosmic Mind - YouTube.” Tevin Naidu. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XaSIs2fp7V8.
Koons, Robert C. 2014. “Staunch Vs. Faint-Hearted Hylomorphism: Toward an Aristotelian Account of Composition.” Res Philosophica 91 (2): 151–77.
Mastropietro, Christopher. 2017. “From George Romero to The Walking Dead: The Meaning of the Zombie.” https://doi.org/10.11647/obp.0173.0065.
Murphy, James (Translator). 1935. Erwin Schrödinger: Science and the Human Temperament. London: George Allen & Unwin LTD. https://doi.org/10.2307/2016569.
Papandreou, Marilù. 2018. “Aristotle’s Hylomorphism and The Contemporary Metaphysics of Artefacts.” Ancient Ontologies. Contemporary Debates, no. Koslicki 2018: 113–36. https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctvvngfp.8.
Pasnau, Robert. 2002. Thomas Aquinas on Human Nature. Cambridge University Press.
Pinchbeck, Daniel. 2024. “Three Dimensions of Time: An Approach for Conceptualizing How Consciousness relates to the Physical World.” Jonathan Schooler. https://vimeo.com/1016331682/4accbedd60.
Rea, Michael C. 2011. “Hylomorphism and the Mind-Body Problem.” Philosophical Perspectives 25 (1): 341–58.
Sayādaw, Venerable Mahāsi. 2013. A Discourse on the Anatta Lakkhaṇa Sutta. Edited by Bhikkhu Pesala. Burma: Socialist Republic of the Union of Burma.
Schrödinger, Erwin. 1950. Space-Time Structure. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
———. 1952. “Are There Quantum Jumps? Part I.” The British Journal for the Philosophy of Science 3 (10): 109–23. https://doi.org/10.1038/2011272a0.
———. 1953. “Are there quantum jumps? Part II.” British Journal for the Philosophy of Science 3 (12): 233–42. https://doi.org/10.1093/bjps/III.12.348.
———. 1954. Nature and the Greeks and Science and Humanism. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. www.cambridge.org/9781107431836.
Stump, Eleonore. 2003. Aquinas. Routledge.
Vervaeke, John. 2013. “Why are zombies so big right now?” https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iSwAbQD-gZU.
———. 2016. “Buddhism and Cognitive Science: Responding to the Meaning Crisis.” Buddhism; Cognitive Science. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uc8wy_4H8X8&list=PLwzqpDoZ6TCKqhjfiXmgxtPB1LLBrBvKd.
Vervaeke, John, Christopher Mastropietro, and Filip Miscevic. 2017. Zombies in Western Culture. A Twenty-First Century Crisis. Cambridge, UK: Open Book Publishers. https://www.openbookpublishers.com/books/10.11647/obp.0113.
